title: Quadratic Equations
learning_objectives: Factor a quadratic of the form x^2 + bx + c.
Solve quadratics using the zero-product property.
Solve x^2 = kx without losing the x = 0 root.
Use the relationships sum of roots = −b and product of roots = c (for monic quadratics).

pedagogical_model.sequence: I do → we do → you do

opening.builds_on: Factoring expressions and solving linear equations.

items.prompt: If (x − 2)(x + 5) = 0, why must a factor be 0?
items.answer: A product equals 0 only if at least one factor is 0.
items.targets: the logic that turns factoring into solving

retrieval_starter.purpose: Retrieves trinomial factoring, difference of squares, and the zero-product property — the three tools that solve almost every GMAT quadratic.
retrieval_starter.if_students_struggle: If factoring stalls, list factor pairs of c and test which pair sums to b.

opening.prior_knowledge_bridge: You can already factor (last lesson) and solve linear equations. Q5 is the hinge that connects them: once a quadratic is factored and set equal to 0, the zero-product property turns it into two linear equations. Today you'll factor, then solve — and learn the trap of dividing by x.
opening.learning_intention: By the end you can solve quadratic equations by factoring and the zero-product property, and read off the sum and product of the roots.
success_criteria: I can factor x^2 + bx + c by finding two numbers with product c and sum b.
I can apply the zero-product property to get both solutions.
I move everything to one side instead of dividing by x.

opening_script.move: Bridge: factor → set each factor to 0 → two linear solves.

i_do.explanation: Two numbers with product +10 and sum −7 are −2 and −5: (x − 2)(x − 5) = 0, so x = 2 or x = 5.
think_aloud_steps: Need two numbers multiplying to +10 and adding to −7 → −2 and −5.
i_do.key_takeaway: For x^2 + bx + c = 0, find two numbers multiplying to c and adding to b; then set each factor to 0.

we_do.explanation: Product −15, sum +2 → +5 and −3: (x + 5)(x − 3) = 0, so x = −5 or x = 3.
scaffold_hints: Find two numbers with product −15 and sum +2.

immediate_feedback.if_incorrect: A negative product means opposite signs; the larger absolute value (5) carries the sign of b (+).

we_do.explanation: Move everything to one side: x^2 − 9x = 0 → x(x − 9) = 0 → x = 0 or x = 9.
scaffold_hints: Don't divide by x — that throws away a root.
Bring all terms to one side: x^2 − 9x = 0.

immediate_feedback.if_incorrect: Dividing by x loses x = 0. Factor instead.

you_do.explanation: Product −14, sum −5 → −7 and +2: (x − 7)(x + 2) = 0, so x = 7 or x = −2.

you_do.stem: If (x − 4)(x + 6) = 0, what is the sum of the solutions?
you_do.explanation: Solutions are x = 4 and x = −6; their sum is −2.

you_do.stem: One solution of x^2 + kx + 12 = 0 is x = 3. What is k?
The two solutions of x^2 − bx + 12 = 0 are positive integers. Which of the following could be the value of b?
you_do.explanation: Positive-integer roots multiplying to 12 are (1,12), (2,6), (3,4), giving b = sum = 13, 8, or 7. Only 7 appears.

sample_held_out_items.explanation: Product −6, sum −1 → −3 and +2: (x − 3)(x + 2) = 0, so x = 3 or x = −2.

sample_held_out_items.stem: What is the product of the solutions of x^2 − 9x + 20 = 0?
sample_held_out_items.explanation: For x^2 + bx + c, the product of the roots is c = 20 (the roots are 4 and 5).

mastery_check.definition: A topic is mastered at >=85% correct on new/held-out-style questions across >=2 spaced sessions with delay (PRD Section 9.2). Flashcard recall alone is not mastery; application under mixed, delayed conditions is the bar.
conditions: >=85% correct on new/held-out-style items (not flashcard recall) per session
sustained across >=2 spaced sessions with a delay between them
per-topic memory surfaced only after >=20 graded reviews (PRD give-up rule)
per-topic performance surfaced only after >=8 application attempts (PRD give-up rule)
mastery_check.on_fail: Not yet: route every miss to the required error log (tag why: content gap / reasoning flaw / timing / careless / trap), schedule spaced re-practice via topic-aware scheduling, then re-enter the we-do -> you-do loop before re-testing.
